name: Linked List Reversal — Full, Partial, K-Groups

metadata.career_relevance: Reverse Linked List is the #1 most common linked list interview question. Reverse in K-Groups (LeetCode #25) is a Flipkart/Amazon favourite for mid-level SDE roles. Mastering reversal unlocks reorder list, palindrome check, and many other problems.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Zerodha batch order processing:
Orders arrive: A→B→C→D→E
Process in reverse (LIFO): E→D→C→B→A

3-pointer reversal — O(n) O(1):
  prev=null, curr=head, next=null
  while curr≠null:
    next = curr.next    (save)
    curr.next = prev    (reverse)
    prev = curr         (advance prev)
    curr = next         (advance curr)
  return prev  ← new head
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Full reversal, reverse between positions, reverse in k-groups — all in O(n) O(1).

visual_aid.description: Step-by-step reversal of 1→2→3→null. State at each step: prev/curr/next arrows. Step 0: prev=null,curr=1,next=?. Step 1: save next=2, reverse 1.next=null, prev=1,curr=2. Step 2: save next=3, reverse 2.next=1, prev=2,curr=3. Step 3: save next=null, reverse 3.next=2, prev=3,curr=null. Return prev=3. Result: 3→2→1→null.
visual_aid.alt_text: Three-pointer linked list reversal step by step

worked_example.problem_statement: Reverse every k nodes. If remaining < k, leave as is.

step_by_step_solution.details: Check if k nodes remain. If yes: reverse k nodes, connect tail to result of recursive call. If no: return head as is.

things_to_notice: head becomes tail after reversal — connect it to next group
prev becomes the new head of the reversed group
Recursion depth: O(n/k) levels — not O(n) like naive recursion
count<k check: leave partial group unchanged

industry_examples.use_case: Process orders in batches of k, each batch in reverse priority. Reverse K-Group in O(n) O(1) space.

applications.interview_relevance: 'Reverse a LL' in 5 minutes: 3-pointer, O(n) O(1). 'Reverse between positions': use dummy + reversal on sublist. 'K-Group': count + reverse + recurse.
applications.real_world_workflow: Reversal blueprint: (1) identify reversal range, (2) save connection points (prev of start, next of end), (3) reverse the sublist, (4) reconnect.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: After reversing, forgetting to connect the old head (new tail) to the rest of the list. head.next = nextGroup is essential.
common_gaps.remediation: Before reversing: save tail = head (the current head becomes the tail after reversal). After reversing: tail.next = nextGroup. The head-becomes-tail pattern is the key connection point.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student reverses correctly but produces disconnected list

key_takeaways: head becomes tail after reversal — always connect it
Reverse between [m,n]: dummy head + reverse [m..n] sublist
K-Group: count k, reverse, head.next = reverseKGroup(rest, k)

self_check_questions.question: After reversing a linked list, why do we return prev and not curr?
self_check_questions.answer: At the end of the while loop, curr is null (we've processed all nodes). prev holds the last node we processed — which is the original LAST node of the list. That last node is now the NEW HEAD because all pointers have been reversed. curr is null so returning it would give us nothing. Returning prev gives us the new head of the reversed list.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Cycle Detection — Floyd's algorithm, finding cycle length, finding cycle entry point.
